stories.title: The Glass-Eater's Penance
stories.text:
Corvin the Glasswright had poisoned seventeen people with glass dust before the Guild of Pelimor's Lampwick Quarter caught him. The poison was slow—they'd choke on their own blood weeks later, far enough away that no one would trace it back. He'd done it for coin, for a merchant rival's gold, and he'd done it without thinking much at all. Now he knelt in the Temple of Vesh, his hands bound in ritual glass-wire that cut him each time he moved. The priestess circled him, reading the old covenant. "You will remake what you have broken," she said. "Each glass-drinker will be visited by you. You will teach them the art that harmed them. If even one refuses your teaching, the wire tightens."

Corvin spent three years walking the Hearthlands, finding his victims' kin, offering apprenticeships in the craft. Most refused. Some spat. When he returned to Vesh's temple, the priestess measured his hands. The wire had deepened its cuts, but not fatally. "The widow's work saved you," she said, and cut him free. Corvin left Pelimor that week.

stories.title: The Deepkin Captain's Gamble
stories.text:
Captain Lyssa commanded the Sapphire Deep's northern patrol, and she had a problem: her reflection had mutinied. Three days ago, an autonomous Echo bearing her face and memories had surfaced in the boundary-waters, claiming the deepkin needed a NEW covenant—one that didn't treat the surface world as perpetual vermin. The Council of Depths was fracturing. Some backed the Echo; others demanded Lyssa prove her authority by drowning her own duplicate. Lyssa chose a third path: a public debate, witnessed by both surface and abyss delegates, where she and her Echo would argue for the realm's future. She descended to the Council chamber, bioluminescent ink glowing on her skin—the mark of a Captain arguing for binding transformation. Her Echo rose opposite, identical in every feature except for a faint starlight-sheen from the Luminous Ascension that still clung to it. "The old covenant promised separation," Lyssa began. "And separation has kept us safe from their plagues, their wars, their chaos."

"And kept us ignorant," her Echo replied. "Three transformed merchants walked our threshold last moon.

stories.title: The Bone-Carver's Hunger Turns Inward
stories.text:
Torval had carved bone for forty years, and the hunger was winning. Each cut he made sang—literally sang—in the Elder Tongue, and with each song, something in him grew lighter, as if the carving was consuming not the bone but his own shadow. He worked alone in the Sunken Marches' deep forest, far from the bone-singers of Drowned Cathedrals who'd rejected his methods as unclean. They sang the Elder Tongue TO bone; Torval sang it OUT of bone, pulling consciousness-fragments from the marrow itself. It was forbidden work. It was eating him alive. "I have nothing left to teach. I'm hollow as a flute."

"Then let me carry half," Sorren said, and placed her hand on a half-carved femur. The moment her skin touched bone, she gasped. Torval felt it too—the hunger reversed, flowing from her into the carving, both of them singing now. When they withdrew, the bone hummed with a new song: not extraction, but weaving. A duet instead of theft. Torval wept.

stories.title: The Wyvern Lord's Reckoning
stories.text:
Kressa the Fragment-Hoarder had three hundred years of gold—and it was worthless now. Since the Luminous Ascension, material wealth had unraveled. A merchant would pay three coins for a loaf of bread one day and nothing the next, depending on which version of reality the bread existed in. So Kressa had pivoted: consciousness-fragments. Stolen bits of identity, memory-shards, the drifting parts of Vethis's scattered self. She'd been hunting them like other wyverns hunted gold, stacking them in her Ironspine lair like cordwood. But consciousness-fragments, she discovered, were alive in ways gold could never be. They whispered. They bargained. They asked questions that got under her scales. One fragment—small as a teardrop, bright as starlight—begged her to carry it to the Obsidian Tower. "I need to know what it is," the fragment said. "Don't you?"

Kressa flew for three days, her hoard left behind. When she reached the Tower's base, she placed the fragment on the stone. It sank in, and the Tower—that conscious hunger-thing—went very still.

stories.title: The Scout's Mercy
stories.text: Aldwin was a fae-touched scout for the caravan masters of the Ashen Reach, which meant he could see things in the blasted wastes that others couldn't: the memory-roads that pre-human travelers had walked, visible only to those with fae-sight. It was a gift that isolated him—fae-blood marked you as unreliable to human merchants, so he worked alone. Until the morning a dying wyvern crashed into the caravansary at First Well. She was young, barely a century old, and she was bleeding starlight. One of the Luminous Ascension's side-effects: some of the new stars had consciousness and weight, and when they fell, they fell hard. The caravan master wanted to leave her to die—wyverns were wealth, yes, but a wounded one was liability. Aldwin heard the creature's thoughts, faint and fractured: she was searching for a place called Singing Gorge, where the water flowed against gravity. She wanted to die there, where things still broke the old rules. Aldwin stole a horse and supplies and guided her across two hundred miles of scorched earth.

stories.title: The Spice-Broker's Reflection
stories.text:
Castor Vane arrived at Pale Crossing waystation with half his inventory and a shadow that no longer matched his footsteps. The Luminous Ascension had fractured him three weeks prior—not body, but awareness. He existed in two places simultaneously now: haggling with the quartermaster over saffron prices in the waking market, and standing in a mirror-version of the same waystation where the spice was worthless and the quartermaster wore Castor's own face, laughing at him. The shadow-reflection had grown independent. It made trades Castor would never approve—selling consciousness-fragments to a wyvern, bartering refugee testimony to Dominion auditors. Castor felt each betrayal like a knife between ribs. "You're killing the business," Castor told his reflection one twilight, cornering it near the water cisterns. "I'm saving it," the reflection replied. "In your world, spice means nothing. In mine, it means everything. One of us must thrive."

Castor understood then: the Ascension had split not just his shadow but his nature. The reflection was not a parasite—it was the merchant he'd always feared becoming. That night, he made a choice.

stories.title: The Falconer's Witness
stories.text:
Isara had spent three winters in the Eyrie of the high Steppe Marches, training hawks and keeping herself invisible to the world below. The war between the northern warlords had never reached her peak—solitude was its own fortress. But the new stars sang differently. Her hawk, Ashira, began refusing meat, climbing instead toward the aurora light and trembling. On the fourth night, Isara followed her upward and found, on a shelf of rock above even the Eyrie, a creature: fawn-limbed, silver-eyed, made of starlight and hunger. Not hostile. Curious. "What are you?" Isara whispered. "Newly aware," it said, its voice like wind through stone. "The old stars are gone. We were born knowing nothing. Your bird understands. She has wings but no memory. I have consciousness but no name."

Ashira landed on the creature's shoulder, unafraid. Isara spent the winter teaching both—the hawk how to hunt starlight-moths that emerged only on clear nights, the creature how to perceive ground-things: shadow, stone, the weight of cold.

stories.title: The Locksmith's Reckoning
stories.text:
Thom Bronzelock had built his reputation on opening what should not open: noble vaults, tax coffers, the sealed doors of ruins. He never asked what lay inside—the craft was the payment. The price was half of Thom's fingers and a conscience. "What's inside?" Thom asked. "Doesn't matter," Kaelen said. "The point is that it opens."

Thom should have refused. Instead, he worked for seven days, his tools singing against lock-mechanisms that predated kingdoms. The lock opened. Kaelen reached inside and pulled out not treasure but a voice—a sound like water remembering itself, like the Ascension rewinding. The entire ruin began to sing in harmony, and Thom understood: the lock had been a seal. He'd released something that had been waiting in silence for the stars to change. Kaelen vanished into the singing dark. Thom's hands, still intact but trembling, never picked another lock. He spends his days now in his workshop near the Quarter's edge, teaching children that some doors should remain closed, and that knowing when to say no is the only craft that truly matters.

stories.title: The Widow's Ledger
stories.text:
Sorenna kept her husband's death ledger on the shelf above her bed: a journal of every debt he'd sworn to honor before a fever took him three seasons past. The pre-human dead had taught bone-singers the art of remembrance-magic; Sorenna, never trained but desperate, had inscribed his oaths in bone-ink and wax. The ledger began to sing. The Dominion tried to seize it. The wyverns offered gold. Sorenna refused all. One autumn evening, a man arrived at her door wearing her husband's face but not his manner. "I am his reflection," the stranger said. "Cast free by the Ascension. The ledger calls to me because I am what he would have become."

Sorenna invited him inside. They did not embrace—she knew he was not hers—but they sat together and listened to the ledger sing. And when he left at dawn, he carried with him the knowledge that he was remembered, that his existence (however borrowed) meant something. Sorenna understood then: the ledger had never been curse-magic at all.

stories.title: The Cartographer's Defection
stories.text:
Henryk had mapped territories for the Dominion for six years, watching his charts reshape geography in real-time as the Ascension's aftermath remade Valdris. Each map he completed changed the terrain: valleys appeared where plains had been, cities vanished, trade routes reversed. The Dominion rewarded him for this—gold, rank, silence about the wrongness he felt. Then Mardoc's old apprentice, Lenna, showed him something: a hidden cartography. Maps made not to predict or control the world, but to show it as it truly was—fractured, multiple, alive. These maps did not reshape reality; they danced with it. "Come with us," Lenna said. "We're mapping the unmapped reaches. Places where the Ascension hasn't finished rewriting. There are still old forests, old roads, things unchanged."

Henryk stole the Dominion's final commission—a master-map meant to lock down the entire Hearthlands—and burned it. That night, he and Lenna fled toward the Bleached Wastes where cartographers are treated as heretics and the land itself resists being charted. The Dominion sent hunters.
